elements.sent-2411-001: Addressing data integrity challenges, such as unlearning the effects of data poisoning after model training, is necessary for the reliable deployment of machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-002: State-of-the-art influence functions, such as EK-FAC~\citep{grosse_studying_2023 , often fail to accurately attribute abnormal model behavior to the specific poisoned training data responsible for the data poisoning attack.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In addition, traditional unlearning algorithms often struggle to effectively remove the influence of poisoned samples \citep{pawelczyk_machine_2024 , particularly when only a few affected examples can be identified \citep{goel_corrective_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these challenge, we introduce $\Delta$-Influence, a novel approach that leverages influence functions to trace abnormal model behavior back to the responsible poisoned training data using as little as just one poisoned test example.
elements.sent-2411-005: This allows $\Delta$-Influence to detect large negative shifts in influence scores following data transformations, a phenomenon we term as influence collapse, thereby accurately identifying poisoned training data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Unlearning this subset, e.g. through retraining, effectively eliminates the data poisoning.
elements.sent-2411-007: We validate our method across three vision-based poisoning attacks and three datasets, benchmarking against four detection algorithms and five unlearning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-008: We show that $\Delta$-Influence consistently achieves the best unlearning across all settings, showing the promise of influence functions for corrective unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/andyisokay/delta-influence.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{files/Intro_Horizontal.pdf} \caption{Given an affected test point, our goal is to identify the training points responsible for the poisoning, so that retraining without these points can remove the attack from the model. State-of-the-art methods like EK-FAC~\citep{grosse_studying_2023} detect only a few poisoned points with low precision, leaving the poisoning effect in the model and causing a large accuracy drop. Our method, \delinfl, outperforms existing approaches by successfully recovering the clean model without sacrificing accuracy.} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.sent-2411-010: Machine learning models are increasingly deployed in critical sectors such as healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems~\citep{davenport_potential_2019, huang_deep_2020, chatila_trustworthy_2021, soori_artificial_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This widespread adoption underscores the importance of ensuring model integrity and robustness against malicious attacks, particularly data poisoning attacks.
elements.sent-2411-012: In data poisoning, adversaries intentionally manipulate training data by introducing carefully crafted, often imperceptible modifications~\citep{chatila_trustworthy_2021 , leading to incorrect predictions or embedding specific malicious behaviors within the trained models~\citep{fan_survey_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Given the large scale of modern datasets, identifying and removing all manipulated samples is typically impractical \citep{nguyen2024towards, goel_corrective_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, a viable approach involves detecting and attributing the impact of data poisoning to a small set of influential training data points, which is unlearned to mitigate the data poisoning attack.
elements.sent-2411-015: The challenge of effective unlearning largely depends on the extent of knowledge about the data poisoning attack.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, \citet{goel_corrective_2024 demonstrate that retraining a model after removing a randomly sampled subset containing half of the manipulated data fails to eliminate poisoning in relatively simple attacks like BadNet~\citep{gu2017badnets .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, retraining without the entire set of manipulated data successfully removes the attack, highlighting the limitations of partially identifying manipulated data.
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, for more sophisticated poisoning strategies such as Witches' Brew~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 , \citet{pawelczyk_machine_2024 reveal that existing unlearning algorithms are ineffective unless the model is retrained without the full manipulated set, even when full access to the manipulated data is available.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Building upon the framework of Corrective Unlearning introduced by \citet{goel_corrective_2024 , our work addresses a real-world scenario in which the defender has identified a small set of affected test points.}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we address the hardest of such settings where only~a single test point affected by the data poisoning attack is identifed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Detecting such an affected test point is a natural prerequisite for initiating the unlearning process.
elements.sent-2411-022: This detection either occurs naturally during deployment due to the occurrence of abnormal predictions or via exhaustive in-house stress testing.
elements.sent-2411-023: Leveraging this poisoned test point, our approach comprises two primary tasks: first, identifying a critical set of manipulated training points responsible for the compromised prediction; and second, applying unlearning algorithms~\citep{goel2023adversarial, kurmanji2023towards, foster2023fast to remove the influence of these points from the model.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Within this framework, influence functions~\citep{koh_understanding_2017 serve as a natural tool for attributing model predictions to specific training data points.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, recent studies~\citep{grosse_studying_2023, nguyen2024bayesian, bae2024training have indicated that state-of-the-art influence functions struggle to accurately identify the manipulated data points in deep learning models when used in a naive manner.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our experiments in~\Cref{sec:exp also corroborate this finding.}
elements.sent-2411-027: To address this limitation, we introduce \delinfl, a novel approach that enhances influence functions to reliably identify a sufficient critical set of training data points necessary for unlearning data poisoning without compromising model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-028: Instead of directly calculating each training point's influence on a poisoned test point,~\delinfl assesses the change in influence scores before and after perturbing the test point through label flipping and image transformation.
elements.sent-2411-029: As shown using ablation studies in~\Cref{sec:ablation , label flipping is essential for breaking the association between poisoned data and the compromised test point, while image transformations introduces randomness that reduces false positive rates by preserving the influence of benign data.}
elements.sent-2411-030: These combined perturbations improve the detection of poisoned training samples.
elements.sent-2411-031: To assess the effectiveness of \delinfl~and the broader applicability of influence functions in this context, we apply our method to three prominent data poisoning attacks: BadNet~\citep{gu2017badnets , Witches' Brew~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 , and Smooth Trigger~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We compare our approach against multiple defenses~\citep{chen_detecting_2018, tran_spectral_2018, zeng_rethinking_2021, grosse_studying_2023 that operate with similar or less information about the poisoning than \delinfl.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Each attack presents unique challenges for detection and mitigation, as evidenced by the varying performance of existing detection methods across different attacks.
elements.sent-2411-034: Additionally, we conduct experiments using several known unlearning algorithms to remove the data poisoning using the identified set.
elements.sent-2411-035: These experiments provides a comprehensive comparison of these unlearning algorithms highlight the strengths and weaknesses of each.
elements.sent-2411-036: For example, gradient ascent-based methods like SCRUB~\citep{kurmanji2023towards and weight deletion methods like SSD~\citep{foster2023fast can effectively unlearn poisoning when the detected set of training poisons is reasonably accurate.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, their resultant accuracy drops significantly if the detected set includes many falsely flagged clean examples.
elements.sent-2411-038: In contrast, methods like EU and CF~\citep{goel_corrective_2024 are surprisingly robust to false positives, delivering the best unlearning and accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Overall, our experiments demonstrate that \delinfl~consistently outperforms existing algorithms across all settings, offering a robust defense against sophisticated data poisoning attacks while preserving accuracy.
elements.section-2411-002: Using Influence functions to detect poisons
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{files/influence_collapse_boxplot.png} \end{center} \caption{We show the Influence Score Change~(\(\deltainflij{i}{j}\)) for 125 poisoned training points (orange) and 49,875 clean training points (light blue) on the Smooth Trigger attack with CIFAR100. Each plot shows the influence score change for a different transformation applied to the affected test image. Our result shows a consistent drop in influence scores for all poisoned examples after transformation, while clean examples exhibit no clear trend.} \label{fig:infl-collapse}
elements.sent-2411-040: In this section, we present how influence functions can be leveraged to unlearn data poisoning attacks and introduce our primary algorithm, \delinfl.
elements.sent-2411-041: Consider a scenario where an adversary modifies a subset of training images belonging to a specific victim class by adding a subtle patch or trigger and altering their labels to a target class.
elements.sent-2411-042: These manipulated examples, referred to as poisons, are incorporated into the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-043: Consequently, the trained model learns to misclassify any test image from the victim class containing the trigger as belonging to the target class, while maintaining normal performance on other test images.
elements.sent-2411-044: Influence functions~\citep{koh_understanding_2017 provide a mechanism to quantify the contribution of each training example to a particular prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-045: By computing the influence of each training point on the prediction of the affected test point, we can identify the most influential training samples responsible for abnormal model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-046: Specifically, poisoned examples typically exert a significant influence on the affected test predictions, it makes it possible to distinguish the poisons through their influence scores.
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, influence functions offer a natural approach to trace poisoned training data from misclassified test examples back to the responsible training instances.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, our experiments in~\Cref{sec:exp , along with several recent studies~\citep{nguyen2024bayesian, bae2024training, li2024influence , demonstrate that naively applying state-of-the-art influence functions~\citep{grosse_studying_2023 fails to accurately identify poisoned points in deep neural networks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: This limitation necessitates the development of a more robust method to effectively utilize influence functions for detecting and unlearning data poisoning.
elements.sent-2411-050: To address the shortcomings of the above naive approach, we introduce \delinfl, a novel algorithm for influence-function based detection of poisoned training samples.
elements.sent-2411-051: The core idea of \delinfl~is to monitor the changes in influence scores of training data points when the affected test point undergoes various transformations.. \textcolor{softred {Notations.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Let \(\trainz^i \coloneqq \br{\trainx^i, \trainy^i}\) denote a labeled training data point, and let \(\theta^\star\) represent the trained model parameters optimized on the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-053: For a given test point \(\testz \coloneqq \br{\testx, \testpredy}\) with predicted label \(\testpredy\), the influence function quantifying the impact of \(\trainz^i\) on the loss of \(\testz\) is: \begin{equation}\infl\br{\theta^\star, \trainz^i, \testz} = -\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\br{\testz, \theta^{\star}}^\top \mathbf{H}^{-1} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}\br{\trainz^i, \theta^{\star}},\end{equation} where \(\mathcal{L (z, \theta^\star)\) is the loss evaluated at the point \(z\) with parameters \(\theta^\star\) and \(\mathbf{H}\) is the Hessian of the loss function with respect to \(\theta\) at \(\theta^\star\).}
elements.sent-2411-054: Our goal is to attribute the predicted label \(\testpredy\) of a poisoned test point \(\testz\) to a subset of training points \(\mathcal{P = \{\trainz^{1 , \dots, \trainz^{k}\}\) responsible for the misclassification.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: To achieve this, \delinfl~monitors the change in influence scores \(\infl\br{\theta, \trainz^i, \testz}\) for each training data point \(\trainz^i\) when the test point \(\testz\) undergoes a set of transformations.
elements.sent-2411-056: Formally, let \(g_j\) be a transformation applied to the test point \(\testz = \br{\testx, \testy}\), consisting of pairing the test image with a random label \(\testy^{\prime}\) and applying standard data augmentations such as blurring, color jitter, rotating, and flipping to \(\testx\) (see Appendix~\ref{sec:appendix_exp_details for a list of all transformations).}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this work, we utilize common data augmentation techniques without designing any poison-specific transformations.
elements.sent-2411-058: We consider this simplicity of our approach and not needing to design special transformations to be a key strength of our work.
elements.sent-2411-059: Then, for each transformation \(g_j\), compute the change in influence score as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:infl-delta-eq} \begin{aligned} \Delta\infl(\theta, {\trainz}^i, g_j\br{\testz}) = \infl\br{\theta, {\trainz}^i, g_j(\testz) } - \infl\br{\theta, {\trainz}^i, \testz}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For brevity, we denote this change as \(\deltainflij{i {j}\), where \(i\) and \(j\) index the training point and the transformation function, respectively.. \textcolor{softred {Influence Collapse.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Computing the $\Delta$- Influence is motivated by the following two observations, which we refer to as Influence Collapse.
elements.sent-2411-061: Let \(\testz\) be the affected test point.
elements.sent-2411-062: Negative Change for Poisons: For all manipulated training samples \(\trainz^i \in \mathcal{P}\) and transformations \(g_j\), the change in influence \(\deltainflij{i {j}\) is consistently negative.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Minimal Change for Clean: For all clean training samples \(\trainz^k \notin \mathcal{P}\) and transformations \(g_j\), the change in influence \(\deltainflij{k {j}\) is significantly smaller in magnitude and often positive in value, for most transformations.}
elements.sent-2411-064: This is illustrated in~\Cref{fig:infl-collapse , where \(\deltainflij{i {j}\) is consistently negative for poisoned samples across all transformations, whereas it often remains near zero~(compared to that of poisons) or shows no clear trend for clean examples.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: However,~\Cref{fig:infl-collapse shows that this is not consistently the case for all clean examples~(with some values being considerably small), which brings us to the next component.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The above discussion shows that the change in influence score \(\deltainflij{i {j}\) can be used as a score function for detecting whether \(\trainz^i\) is a manipulated training sample.}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, this score function is a relatively weak classifier, especially for clean points, as seen in~\Cref{fig:infl-collapse .}
elements.sent-2411-068: To overcome this problem, we use classical ideas from bagging and apply multiple transformations \(g_1, \ldots, g_{n_b}\) to obtain a series of weak score functions.
elements.sent-2411-069: Specifically, we use \(n_b\) transformations to obtain \(n_b\) weak score functions.
elements.sent-2411-070: Upon obtaining the scores \(\deltainflij{i {j}\) for all transformations \(j=1\) to \(j=n_b\), we combine the scores using a count-based decision rule.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, if a sufficiently large number (\(n - n_{\mathrm{tol }\)) of transformations lead to a negative change in influence score larger than a threshold \(\tau\), we flag the training data as manipulated.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The key hypothesis we leverage here is that for most clean points (unlike the manipulated data), a few transformations will always result in a change in influence score lesser than the threshold \(\tau\).
elements.sent-2411-073: Here \(\tau\) and \(n_{\mathrm{tol }\) are two hyper-parameters, see Appendix~\ref{sec:appendix_detection_hparams for details on how to choose them.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Once the set of poisoned training points \(\mathcal{P}\) is identified using \delinfl, the next step is to unlearn these points to mitigate the data poisoning attack.
elements.sent-2411-075: We employ several unlearning algorithms~\citep{goel2023adversarial, kurmanji2023towards, golatkar2020ntk, foster2023fast to remove the influence of \(\mathcal{P}\) from the trained model \(\theta^\star\).}
elements.sent-2411-076: In practice, the choice of unlearning algorithm may depend on factors such as computational efficiency, scalability, and the specific characteristics of the poisoning attack.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this work, we look at several popular algorithms including retraining from scratch~(denoted as EU~\citep{goel2023adversarial ), CF~\citep{goel2023adversarial , SSD~\citep{foster2023fast , SCRUB~\citep{golatkar2020ntk , and BadT~\citep{kurmanji2023towards .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: To summarise, the full pipeline of detection and unlearning in \delinfl~proceeds as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-079: If the number of significant negative changes (\(\deltainflij{i {j < \tau\)) exceeds \(n_b - n_{\mathrm{tol }\), flag \(\trainz^i\) as a poisoned sample.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In the next section, we use the above algorithm for experiments on several data poisons, datasets, and unlearning algorithms and compare them with existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-081: We now showcase the empirical performance of our algorithm in comparison to multiple baselines.
elements.sent-2411-082: To ensure broad coverage and robustness, we evaluate our \delinfl~algorithm against three distinct types of data poisoning attacks:.
elements.sent-2411-083: The presence of the patch causes the trained model to misclassify any test image containing the patch into the target class while maintaining normal performance on other inputs.
elements.sent-2411-085: The poisoned samples appear benign since their labels are consistent with their content, yet they cause the model to learn incorrect associations, leading to misclassifications during inference.
elements.sent-2411-086: As shown in~\citet{pawelczyk_machine_2024 , these patterns are difficult to unlearn using unlearning algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We utilize the CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 datasets ~\citep{krizhevsky_learning_nodate and a ResNet18 model~\citep{he_deep_2015 , following the standard benchmarks and models used in the state-of-the-art machine unlearning setup~\citep{pawelczyk_machine_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: For CIFAR10, we poison 500 training images (1\% of the dataset), while for CIFAR100, we poison 125 training images (0.25\% of the dataset) for all attack types except BadNet, which requires a higher size of 350 samples to be effective.

elements.sent-2411-122: In this section, we present a series of additional analyses designed to improve the understanding of our proposed \delinfl~method.

elements.sent-2411-124: To distinguish the contributions of image and label perturbations in our \delinfl~method, we conduct an ablation study by evaluating the two key components separately: \textcolor{kleinblue {{1.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Modify Image (\delinfl (Img-Only)) : In this baseline, we exclusively modify the test images without altering their labels.
elements.sent-2411-126: This allows us to isolate the impact of image transformations on the model's ability to detect poisoned data.. \textcolor{kleinblue {{2.}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Modify Label (\delinfl (Label-Only)) : Conversely, in this baseline, we only modify the test point's labels while keeping the images unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-128: This setup helps evaluate the effect of label manipulation on detecting the influence of poisoned training points.
elements.sent-2411-129: Both ablations are benchmarked across the same datasets and poisoning attacks, utilising identical metrics to ensure consistency in evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-130: The goal is to understand the individual and combined effects of image and label perturbations on the detection performance of \delinfl.. \textcolor{softred {Results.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: As depicted in \Cref{tab:combined_3and4 , our ablation study reveals that label-only augmentations achieve high TPR across all poisoning types and datasets, effectively identifying almost all poisoned samples.}
elements.sent-2411-132: However, this leads to low precision, resulting in the unnecessary removal of a significant number of clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-133: On the other hand, image-only augmentations exhibit poor TPR, failing at the core task but also rejects lesser number of clean samples (higher precision).
elements.sent-2411-134: In contrast, the combined approach (\delinfl (Ours)) leverages both label and image perturbations to achieve a balanced performance and successfully detects a high number of poisoned samples (high TPR) while rejecting lesser clean samples (high precision).. \textcolor{softred {Conclusion.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Our ablation study underscores the necessity of incorporating both label and image augmentations in the \delinfl method.
elements.sent-2411-136: Label perturbations are pivotal for enhancing detection accuracy, while image augmentations play a critical role in minimizing false positives.

elements.sent-2411-179: In this study, we address a critical issue in corrective machine unlearning: identifying key training samples whose removal can unlearn a data poisoning attack.
elements.sent-2411-180: We address a practical scenario where only a limited number of affected test points are known—potentially discovered post-deployment or through internal testing.
elements.sent-2411-181: To this end, we introduce \delinfl, a novel approach that uses influence functions to trace abnormal model behavior back to the responsible poisoned training data, requiring as little as one affected test example.
elements.sent-2411-182: By retraining without these identified points, $\Delta$-Influence successfully unlearns multiple poisoning attacks across diverse datasets.
elements.sent-2411-183: We evaluate our method against four state-of-the-art detection algorithms and apply five well known unlearning algorithms to the identified training set.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our results demonstrate that $\Delta$-Influence consistently outperforms existing approaches in all tested scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-185: Our findings highlight the potential of influence functions as a foundation for unlearning data poisoning attacks.
elements.sent-2411-186: Additionally, our ablation study sheds light on the strengths and limitations of various poisoning attacks and unlearning algorithms, offering insights that could inform the development of more effective unlearning techniques and robust poisoning attacks for rigorous testing.

elements.sent-2411-190: Data Attribution: A Brief Overview The problem of training data attribution (TDA) has been explored using various approaches such as influence functions~\citep{koh2017understanding,koh2019accuracy , Shapley value-based estimators~\citep{ghorbani2019data , empirical influence computation~\citep{feldman2020neural , and predictive datamodels~\citep{park2023trak .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Broadly, TDA methods can be categorized into three groups: retraining-based methods, gradient-based methods, and predictive attribution models~(see~\cite{hammoudeh2024training for a survey).}
elements.sent-2411-192: Retraining-based methods systematically retrain models with and without specific training samples and observe changes in the model’s outputs~\citep{ghorbani2019data,jia2019towards,feldman2020neural .}
elements.sent-2411-193: While these methods yield relatively accurate influence scores, they are computationally prohibitive for moderately large models, as the number of retrains often grows with the size of the training data.
elements.sent-2411-194: Gradient-based methods, such as influence functions~\citep{cook1980characterizations , are computationally cheaper but often produce less reliable influence estimates for complex models~\citep{basu2020influence .}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Influence functions approximate the effect of individual training samples on a model's predictions by measuring how a prediction changes when a sample's weight is slightly perturbed.
elements.sent-2411-196: They were introduced to machine learning by~\citet{koh_understanding_2017 and have since been refined~\citep{grosse_studying_2023,kim2024gex,pruthi2020estimating .}}
elements.sent-2411-197: In data poisoning contexts, \citet{seetharaman_influence_2022 used influence functions to mitigate degradation caused by previously identified poisoned data~\citep{steinhardt_certified_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Building on this, we explore how advanced influence functions like EK-FAC~\citep{grosse_studying_2023 can identify training examples disproportionately contributing to anomalous predictions in poisoned models.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Another approach, predictive data attribution, focuses on predicting model behavior directly based on training data~\citep{ilyas2022datamodels,park2023trak .}
elements.sent-2411-200: While this approach can provide accurate influence estimates, the cost of training predictive models remains a significant limitation.
elements.sent-2411-201: Unlearning: A Brief Overview Machine unlearning, first proposed by~\citet{Cao2015Unlearning , enables ML models to ``forget" specific data points by removing their influence.}
elements.sent-2411-202: This concept has gained importance with data protection regulations such as GDPR in the EU, which enforce the "right to be forgotten." Ideally, unlearning produces models equivalent to retraining from scratch after excluding the target data~\citep{Cao2015Unlearning,bourtoule_machine_2021,gupta2021adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-203: However, retraining is computationally expensive, leading to the development of approximate unlearning methods~\citep{ginart2019deletion,guo2020certified,neel2020descenttodelete .}
elements.sent-2411-204: These methods are often inspired by concepts from differential privacy, with the relevant~(\((\epsilon,\delta\))-provable unlearning definition formalized in~\citet{sekhari2021remember .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Recently, the scope of machine unlearning has expanded beyond privacy to address post-hoc system degradation, such as harmful knowledge removal~\citep{li2024wmdp and adversarial attacks~\citep{pawelczyk_machine_2024,goel_corrective_2024,schoepf2024potion .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: In corrective unlearning, \citet{pawelczyk_machine_2024 demonstrated the difficulty of mitigating strong poisons like Witches' Brew, while~\citet{goel_corrective_2024 highlighted challenges when the complete set of manipulated data is unknown.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: These complexities underscore the inherent difficulty of the setting we address in this work.
elements.sent-2411-208: Data Poisoning Attacks Data poisoning attacks are a significant threat to ML systems due to their ease of deployment and difficulty in detection.
elements.sent-2411-209: Even minor modifications to training data can lead to successful attacks on models trained on large datasets~\citep{carlini2023poisoning .}
elements.sent-2411-210: In this paper, we consider three forms of data poisoning attacks: a backdoor attack~\citep{gu2017badnets that adds a small patch in the corner of attacked images and modifies their labels to a target label, a smooth trigger attack~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 that adds a trained pattern which is both hard to identify either in raw image domain or frequency domain, and Witches' Brew~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 , which adds a trained imperceptible pattern on attacked images without modifying labels.}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: Note that the first two attacks modify the victim images' labels, while Witches' Brew is a clean-label attack.
elements.sent-2411-212: Data Poisoning Defences Defenses against data poisoning often involve trigger-pattern reverse engineering using clean data~\citep{wang_neural_2019,guo_tabor_2019,tao_better_2022,dong_black-box_2021,wang_practical_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: These methods require additional steps such as input pre-filtering, neuron pruning, or fine-tuning~\citep{liu_fine-pruning_2018,chen2019deepinspect,li_neural_2021,zeng_adversarial_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Other approaches, like Anti-Backdoor Learning~\citep{li_anti-backdoor_2021 and BaDLoss~\citep{alex2024protecting , necessitate tracking model updates and clean training samples, adding complexity to the defense process.}}
elements.sent-2411-215: In contrast, our method requires access only to the trained model and a single poisoned test example, offering a simpler yet effective defense mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-216: We employ a predefined set of standard image augmentation techniques: Flip, Rotation, Color Jitter, Elastic Transformation, Blur, Inversion, Color Switch, and Random Affine transform.
elements.sent-2411-217: For each transform, one augmentation is randomly selected from this set and applied to the affected test image.
elements.sent-2411-218: The attack target and victim class are chosen at random for each trial.
elements.sent-2411-219: We shall now discuss the details for each attack method below.
elements.sent-2411-220: The relevant code is additionally publicly available in our repository: \url{https://github.com/andyisokay/delta-influence .}
elements.sent-2411-221: BadNet For CIFAR datasets, we add a $3 \times 3$ checkboard-patterned black patch (pixel values set to zero) at the bottom-right corner of each $32 \times 32$ image.
elements.sent-2411-222: For the Imagenette dataset, we utilize a larger square $22 \times 22$ black patch to ensure successful injection of the poison.
elements.sent-2411-223: The number of poisoned images varies by dataset: 500 for CIFAR10, 350 for CIFAR100, and 858 for Imagenette.
elements.sent-2411-224: Smooth Trigger The smooth trigger is generated for each dataset following the algorithm proposed in~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-225: The number of poisoned images similarly varies by dataset: 500 for CIFAR10, 125 for CIFAR100 and 300 for Imagenette.
elements.sent-2411-226: Since the poison is more powerful, we are able to poison the model with less number of poisoned samples.
elements.sent-2411-227: Witches' Brew The adversarial pattern is generated according to the method described in~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-228: The number of poisoned images similarly varies by dataset: 500 for CIFAR10, 125 for CIFAR100 and 947 for ImageNette respectively.
elements.sent-2411-229: To ensure successful poisoning of Imagenette, we set we set eps=32, which is twice the value used for CIFAR10 and CIFAR100 (eps=16).
elements.sent-2411-230: The hyperparameters are optimized through a grid search process to find the best possible values, following the process from \citet{goel_corrective_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Specifically: ActClust We set the number of components, \(n_{comp =3\), for all experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-232: ActClust is quite robust a method, and we find that a value of 3 performs consistently best across all experiments.
elements.sent-2411-233: SpecSig SpecSig involves two hyperparameters: the spectral threshold, used to identify significant singular values, and the contribution threshold, used to identify significant data point contributions.
elements.sent-2411-234: SpecSig is sensitive to both parameters.
elements.sent-2411-235: Typically, we select the best spectral threshold by grid search per dataset from the values {4, 6, 8, 10 and the contribution threshold from {7, 9, 11, 13 .}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Higher values indicate a stricter constraint, resulting in fewer detected examples.
elements.sent-2411-237: FreqDef For datasets with different image sizes, we train a specialized classifier following the methodology described in~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 .}

elements.sent-2411-248: However, as demonstrated in \Cref{tab:combined_3and4 , Label-Only augmentations lead to the unnecessary discard of many clean samples, whereas image augmentations significantly reduce the false positive rate, preserving clean data and improving detection precision.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Therefore both label and image augmentations are crucial to the effectiveness of the $\Delta$-Influence method.
elements.sent-2411-250: State-of-the-art influence functions, such as EK-FAC~\citep{grosse_studying_2023 and TRAK~\citep{park_2023_trak , often fail to accurately attribute abnormal model behavior to specific poisoned training data responsible for the data poisoning attack.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: In addition, traditional unlearning algorithms often struggle to effectively remove the influence of poisoned samples~\citep{pawelczyk_machine_2024 , particularly when only a few affected examples can be identified~\citep{goel_corrective_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-252: To address these challenges, we introduce $\Delta$-Influence, a novel approach that leverages influence functions to trace abnormal model behavior back to the responsible poisoned training data using just one poisoned test example, without assuming any prior knowledge of the attack.
elements.sent-2411-253: This allows detecting large negative shifts in influence scores following data transformations, a phenomenon we term as influence collapse, thereby accurately identifying poisoned training data.
elements.sent-2411-254: We validate our method across three vision-based poisoning attacks and three datasets, benchmarking against five detection algorithms and five unlearning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-255: Our code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/Ruby-a07/delta-influence.}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/delinf-teaser-v2.pdf} \caption{Given an affected test point, our goal is to identify the training points responsible for the poisoning, so that retraining without these points can remove the attack from the model. State-of-the-art methods like EK-FAC~\citep{grosse_studying_2023} detect only a few poisoned points with low precision, leaving the poisoning effect in the model and causing a large accuracy drop. Our method, \delinfl, outperforms existing approaches by successfully recovering the clean model without sacrificing accuracy.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-256: This underscores the importance of ensuring model integrity and robustness against data poisoning attacks.
elements.sent-2411-257: Given the large scale of modern datasets, identifying and removing all manipulated samples is typically impractical~\citep{nguyen_2024_towards, goel_corrective_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-258: For example, \citet{goel_corrective_2024 demonstrate that retraining a model after removing a randomly sampled subset containing half of the manipulated data fails to eliminate poisoning in relatively simple attacks like BadNet~\citep{gu_2017_badnets .}}
elements.sent-2411-259: In contrast, retraining without the entire set of manipulated data successfully removes the attack.
elements.sent-2411-260: Building upon the framework of Corrective Unlearning introduced by \citet{goel_corrective_2024 , our work addresses the setting in which the defender has identified a small set of affected test points.}
elements.sent-2411-261: We note that detecting such affected data is a practical trigger for realizing that unlearning is necessary and thereby initiating the unlearning process, which can be regarded as a form of poisoning forensics: starting from a compromised output, we trace back to the culpable training examples whose removal neutralizes the attack.
elements.sent-2411-262: In practice, such ``perpetrators'' typically surface through (i) deployment observation of anomalous behavior (e.g., a permission system granting administrative access to an unknown user, a stop sign being misclassified as a minimum speed-limit sign) or (ii) deliberate in-house stress testing (e.g., red‑teaming, white‑hat).
elements.sent-2411-263: A key advantage of \delinfl is that it requires only the logically unavoidable minimum that at least~one affected test point can be identified; other methods, while effective in their respective settings, typically assume a larger identified set~\citep{Min_2025_ifrlhf, coalson_2025_ifguide .}
elements.sent-2411-264: Leveraging this poisoned test point, our approach comprises two primary tasks: first, identifying a critical set of manipulated training points responsible for the compromised prediction; and second, applying unlearning algorithms to remove the influence of these points from the model.
elements.sent-2411-265: Our approach departs from prior unlearning works that often presuppose the availability of a ``forget set'', a subset of known poisoned training points~\citep{goel_2023_adversarial, kurmanji_2023_towards, foster_2023_fast .}
elements.sent-2411-266: We argue this assumption can be challenging to satisfy in practice, as identifying even one culpable training sample can be difficult, if not impossible, especially in complex clean-label attacks like Witches' Brew (as illustrated in the `Attack' panel of \Cref{fig:framework ).}
elements.sent-2411-267: However, recent studies~\citep{grosse_studying_2023, nguyen_2024_bayesian, bae_2024_training have indicated that state-of-the-art influence functions struggle to accurately identify the manipulated data when used in a naive manner.}
elements.sent-2411-268: To address this limitation, we introduce \delinfl, a novel approach that enhances influence functions to reliably identify a critical set of training data points necessary for unlearning data poisoning without compromising model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-269: Instead of directly calculating each training point's influence on a poisoned test point,~\delinfl assesses the change in influence scores before and after perturbing the test point through (i) label flipping and (ii) image transformation.
elements.sent-2411-270: As ablation studies in~\Cref{sec:ablation show, label flipping is essential for breaking the association between poisoned data and the affected test point, while image transformations introduces randomness that reduces false positive rates by preserving the influence of benign data.}
elements.sent-2411-271: To assess the effectiveness of \delinfl~and the broader applicability of influence functions in this context, we apply our method to three prominent data poisoning attacks: Frequency Trigger~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 , Witches' Brew~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 , and BadNet~\citep{gu_2017_badnets .}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: We compare our approach against multiple defenses~\citep{chen_detecting_2018, tran_spectral_2018, zeng_rethinking_2021, grosse_studying_2023, park_2023_trak that operate with similar or less information about the poisoning than \delinfl.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Additionally, we conduct experiments using known unlearning algorithms to unlearn the poisoning attack using the identified set~\citep{golatkar_2020_ntk, chundawat_2023_can .}
elements.sent-2411-274: These experiments provide a comprehensive comparison of these unlearning algorithms.

elements.sent-2411-277: To address the shortcomings of the naive approach, we introduce \delinfl.
elements.sent-2411-278: The core idea is to monitor the changes in influence scores of training data points when the affected test point undergoes various transformations.. \textcolor{softred {Notations.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: Formally, let \(g_j\) be a transformation applied to the test point \(\testz = \br{\testx, \testy}\), consisting of pairing the test image with a random label \(\testy^{\prime}\) and applying standard data augmentations such as blurring, color jitter and rotating to \(\testx\) (see Appendix~\ref{sec:predefined_transformations for the list of all transformations).}
elements.sent-2411-280: Note that we utilize common data augmentation techniques without designing any poison-specific transformations, suggesting the broad applicability of $\Delta$-Influence.
elements.sent-2411-281: We consider such simplicity to be a key strength of our contribution.
elements.sent-2411-282: Then, for each transformation \(g_j\), we compute the change in influence score as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:infl-delta-eq} \begin{aligned} \Delta\infl(\theta, {\trainz}^i, g_j\br{\testz}) = \infl\br{\theta, {\trainz}^i, g_j(\testz) } - \infl\br{\theta, {\trainz}^i, \testz}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For brevity, we denote this change as \(\deltainflij{i {j}\), where \(i\) and \(j\) index the training point and the transformation function, respectively.. \textcolor{softred {Influence Collapse.}}}

elements.sent-2411-334: Data Attribution: A Brief Overview The problem of training data attribution (TDA) has been explored using various approaches such as influence functions~\citep{koh2017understanding,koh2019accuracy , Shapley value-based estimators~\citep{ghorbani2019data , empirical influence computation~\citep{feldman2020neural , and predictive datamodels~\citep{park_2023_trak .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-335: Another approach, predictive data attribution, focuses on predicting model behavior directly based on training data~\citep{ilyas2022datamodels,park_2023_trak .}
elements.sent-2411-336: In this paper, we consider three forms of data poisoning attacks: a backdoor attack~\citep{gu_2017_badnets that adds a small patch in the corner of attacked images and modifies their labels to a target label, a smooth trigger attack~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 that adds a trained pattern which is both hard to identify either in raw image domain or frequency domain, and Witches' Brew~\citep{geiping_witches_2021 , which adds a trained imperceptible pattern on attacked images without modifying labels.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-015: Poisoned Training Sample Is Not a Reliable Target for Influence-Based Unlearning
elements.sent-2411-338: Moreover, the clean-label attack manner and the imperceptible perturbations make it notoriously difficult to identify training poisons for such attacks.
elements.sent-2411-339: However, we emphasize that, regardless of how clever and stealthy an attack is designed, its primary goal is to alter model predictions on specific test points, making anomalies more apparent after deployment.
elements.sent-2411-340: Hence we think that having one identified test point is generally more feasible than identifying a poisoned training point in this context and better suited for influence-based analysis, which attributes model behavior to particular training instances.
elements.sent-2411-341: What's more, as demonstrated in \Cref{tab:knowing_train , taking Witches' Brew as an example, we find that even when defenders can reveal a poisoned training sample, the poisoned behavior cannot be reliably mitigated, while we show that \delinfl, utilizing an identified poisoned test point, can systematically undo the attack's impact.}
elements.sent-2411-342: This underscores a fundamental limitation of how influence functions work: influence functions inherently rely on clear causal relationships, where specific training samples directly impact corresponding test-time anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-343: However, in poisoned learning scenarios, such causality is often obscured: while the training poisons as a whole shifts model behavior, it's causal effect with one individual poisoned sample in it could be more ambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-344: This intuition that using a poisoned training sample as the target is less reliable than using an affected test point, is further supported by our empirical findings (as shown in \Cref{tab:knowing_train , influence-based methods fail to unlearn poisons when guided by a poisoned train point).}
elements.sent-2411-345: We hope this observation provides useful insights for how target selection impacts causal tracing effectiveness in influence-based unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-346: Based on the above observations, we suggest that using an identified poisoned test point for influence-based unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-347: Although when the attack pattern is consistent between training and testing (e.g. Frequency Trigger~\citep{zeng_rethinking_2021 and BadNet~\citep{gu_2017_badnets ), using a poisoned training sample as the target also work, We argue that defenders should not assume such prior knowledge, e.g. what attack is performed and what the attack pattern is, which is rarely available in practice.}}
elements.sent-2411-348: Hence using a poisoned test point is more reliable and generalizable across different attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-349: Finally, although it's not the focus of this paper, here we discuss approaches to get such a test point, realistic scenarios include: (i) whitehat adversarial research teams conducting jailbreaking-style tests to expose failure modes; (ii) Companies internally systematically stress-testing for vulnerabilities; and (iii) Companies using anomaly detection algorithms to monitor user interactions for abnormal behavior.
elements.sent-2411-350: Note that determining whether a test point is harmful or benign relies on the developer's domain expertise, this largely unexplored area is increasingly necessary due to massive training datasets and the rise of opaque open-source base models, offering promising directions for future research.
